name: Ivan T. Sanderson
description: Ivan Terence Sanderson (1911-1973) was a British-American zoologist and writer who became a prominent figure in cryptozoology, ufology, and fortean phenomena. He is best known for his books exploring unexplained mysteries, including potential evidence of undiscovered or misidentified animals (cryptids), anomalous zones of electromagnetic disturbance (vile vortices), and unusual atmospheric phenomena. Sanderson co-founded the Society for the Investigation of the Unexplained (SITU), an organization dedicated to studying such topics. His claims often involved extrapolating from anecdotal evidence, historical accounts, and his own observations, leading to speculative theories about the existence of creatures like the Mothman, the Minnesota Iceman, and various sea serpents. Mainstream science generally dismisses Sanderson's cryptozoological claims as lacking rigorous scientific evidence and relying on unsubstantiated reports or misinterpretations of known phenomena. His work, while influential within fringe science circles, is largely considered pseudoscience due to its lack of adherence to the scientific method and its tendency to prioritize anecdotal evidence over empirical data. His 'vile vortices' theory, proposing twelve areas of unusual electromagnetic activity around the globe, has been similarly criticized for lacking scientific basis and relying on selective data to support a predetermined conclusion.
subtitle: Cryptozoology, Vile Vortices, Unexplained Phenomena

claims.evidence: Anecdotal reports, blurry photographs, footprints, indigenous folklore.
claims.criticism: Lack of verifiable physical evidence, misidentification of known species, hoaxes.

claims.claim: Presence of 'vile vortices,' areas of increased paranormal activity.
claims.evidence: Disappearances, electromagnetic anomalies, strange weather patterns in specific geographic locations.
claims.criticism: Lack of controlled studies, cherry-picking of data, alternative explanations for observed phenomena.

claims.claim: Unexplained aerial phenomena are often extraterrestrial in origin.
claims.evidence: Eyewitness accounts of UFO sightings, historical reports of unusual aerial objects.
claims.criticism: Psychological biases in eyewitness testimony, misidentification of conventional aircraft or natural phenomena, lack of verifiable physical evidence.

keyFigures.name: Ivan T. Sanderson
keyFigures.role: Proponent and researcher of cryptozoology and fortean phenomena

keyFigures.name: Philip J. Klass
keyFigures.role: Prominent UFO skeptic and critic of Sanderson's claims

timeline.event: Ivan T. Sanderson born in Edinburgh, Scotland.
Sanderson co-founds the Society for the Investigation of the Unexplained (SITU).
Ivan T. Sanderson dies in New Jersey, USA.
